questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper reports pre-training on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also evaluates transfer performance on smaller benchmarks including ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed is DeiT-B★, which has 87M parameters. The paper’s reference vision transformer is 86M, but the biggest variant in the provided evidence is 87M.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention only within non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, and this design yields linear computational complexity with respect to image size. Relative position bias is another architectural choice highlighted as beneficial.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer architecture based on self-distillation without labels. A student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, using centering and sharpening to prevent collapse; it is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, though the paper highlights strong results for ViTs with small patches.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a wide spread in reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy, from 1.3 to 91.0 (stddev 8.69), so papers do not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: CoCa (finetuned) is listed at 91.0, while other papers report lower top claims such as PaLI-17B at 90.9, CoCa (frozen) at 90.6, and MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The evidence does not list any explicit SOTA claims for this dataset, though the highest reported value is 91.88% (AdaptFormer-Swin).

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus reports a wide range of ADE20K mIoU values, from 26.6 to 62.9 (spread 36.3, stddev 7.29), so the papers do not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: InternImage-H is listed at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L is listed at 61.5 and SwinV2-G at 59.9.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows mixed views: some papers treat position embeddings as part of the standard ViT design, e.g. ViT says it “adds position embeddings,” and CPVT says vision transformers “follow the same fashion to imbue the network with positional information.” But several papers argue they are not strictly necessary or can be removed with little harm, such as CvT (“can safely remove positional encoding without degradation”) and the self-supervised ViT study (“Removing position embedding only slightly hurts performance”). Other papers instead emphasize replacing absolute embeddings with relative/conditional biases rather than eliminating positional information altogether.

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy rose from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. Overall, the trend is up at first, peaking in 2022, followed by a downward trend through 2024.

questions.question: What is the trend in average model parameter count by year in this corpus?
